title: Chicken casserole with cider and tarragon
subtitle: A light, fragrant braise with dry cider and fresh tarragon
excerpt: Chicken pieces braised in dry cider with shallots and tarragon make a quick weeknight casserole that tastes far more considered than the effort required. Serve it with mashed potato to soak up the pale, anise-scented sauce.
sourceNotes: This combination of chicken, cider, and tarragon draws on the Normandy tradition of poulet au cidre, adapted for British home cooking. The French original uses cream; this British supper version keeps it lighter. Method informed by general British weeknight braise conventions.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The casserole keeps well in the fridge for 2 days. The sauce may need a splash of water on reheating as it thickens.

glossaryTerms.definition: Simmering a liquid to evaporate water and concentrate its flavour and body. A reduced cider sauce becomes thicker and less sharp.

content.text: Chicken with cider and tarragon is a light weeknight braise: chicken thighs and shallots cooked in dry cider until the sauce is just thick enough to coat a spoon. It takes about an hour and uses ingredients most kitchens carry.

The key is using dry cider rather than sweet. Sweet cider turns cloying as it reduces. Dry cider loses its sharpness and leaves behind a clean, slightly fruity base that works with the anise of the tarragon.
Season the chicken thighs generously with salt and pepper on both sides. Heat the oil in a wide casserole dish over a medium-high heat.
Brown the chicken, skin-side down, for 5 to 6 minutes until golden and the skin has crisped. Turn and brown the other side for 2 minutes. Remove and set aside.
Pour off all but a thin film of fat from the casserole. Add the shallots, cut-side down, and cook over a medium heat for 4 to 5 minutes until golden. Add the garlic and cook for 1 minute more.
Pour in the cider and let it bubble for 2 minutes,
slightly. Add the stock, mustard, and tarragon stalks. Stir.
Return the chicken, skin-side up, so the skin sits above the liquid. Cover and simmer over a low heat for 35 to 40 minutes until the chicken is cooked through.
Lift out the chicken. Discard the tarragon stalks. If using cream, stir it into the sauce now. Simmer uncovered for 5 minutes until the sauce has a light coating consistency.
Return the chicken to the pot. Scatter over the fresh tarragon leaves and serve with mashed potato or steamed rice.

items.cause: Too much liquid was used, or the lid was on the whole time without any reduction.
items.fix: Lift out the chicken, remove the lid, and simmer the sauce briskly for 5 to 8 minutes to reduce it.

items.cause: The skin went into the liquid during cooking.
items.fix: Always position chicken skin-side up so it stays above the sauce. The next time, crispen it briefly under the grill for 3 minutes before serving.

items.cause: The sauce did not reduce enough, or sweet cider was used instead of dry.
items.fix: Simmer longer to mellow the sharpness. A teaspoon of cream stirred in at the end softens it further.

items.symptom: The tarragon has lost its brightness
items.cause: It was added too early and cooked through with the braise.
items.fix: The fresh leaves should go on at the very end, off the heat. Next time reserve all the fresh leaves for the finish.

content.text: Chicken with cider is a dish the British borrowed from the French and made their own. Normandy's poulet vallee d'Auge uses cream and calvados; the British version simplifies it into a weeknight supper, using whatever dry cider sits in the fridge. Tarragon travels from the French tradition but has been at home in British cooking long enough to feel native.
